segments.inferno-4-g12: The explicit subject “Io” is emphatic: Virgil says that he himself will take the lead. “Sarò” is the future tense of “essere,” and “primo” is a predicate adjective/noun meaning “first” in order.
segments.inferno-4-g13: “E” coordinates the second half with the first, setting up a parallel contrast between “io” and “tu.” “Sarai” is the second-person singular future of “essere,” and “secondo” here means second in order, not “according to.”
segments.inferno-4-g14: “E io” resumes the narration with an explicit subject, “And I,” preparing for the main verb “dissi” later in the sentence. The subject is delayed by the intervening relative clause.
segments.inferno-4-g15: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring back to “io”: “I, who….” “Mi fui accorto” is a compound past form of the pronominal verb “accorgersi,” meaning “had noticed”; “del color” depends on it, meaning “of the color/pallor,” though the supplied sense for “color” does not match this use.
segments.inferno-4-g16: “Dissi” is the first-person singular past definite of “dire,” the main verb of the narrative frame: “I said.” It follows the delayed subject “io” after the relative clause.
segments.inferno-4-g17: “Come” introduces a direct question meaning “how.” “Verrò” is future tense, literally “will I come,” but in context it has the sense “how shall I proceed/go on?”
segments.inferno-4-g18: “Se tu paventi” is a conditional clause, “if you fear/are afraid”; the listed sense for “se” is not appropriate here. “Che suoli … esser conforto” is a relative clause referring to “tu”: “you who are wont to be comfort”; “al mio dubbiare” uses the infinitive as a noun, “to my doubting.”
segments.inferno-4-g19: This segment contains only punctuation, marking the end of the preceding quoted question. There are no words here to analyze for grammar or sense.
segments.inferno-4-g20: “Ed” is the coordinating conjunction “and,” with the -d added before a vowel sound. “Elli” is an archaic form of “egli,” meaning “he,” despite the supplied senses; “a me” means “to me,” introducing his reply.
segments.inferno-4-g21: “L’angoscia” is the subject of the verb that comes in the next segment, “dipigne.” “De le genti” is the older separated spelling of modern “delle genti,” “of the people,” and “che son qua giù” is a relative clause meaning “who are down here.”
segments.inferno-4-g22: “Dipigne” is an archaic/poetic form of “dipinge,” present third singular: “paints.” The subject is still “L’angoscia,” while “nel viso mi” means “on my face,” with “mi” functioning possessively/datively; “quella pietà che tu per tema senti” means “that pity which you perceive as fear.”
segments.inferno-4-g23: “Andiam” is an abbreviated first-person plural present form of “andare,” used here as an exhortation: “let us go.” The imperative force comes from the context and the first-person plural form.
segments.inferno-4-g24: “ché” introduces the reason for the command, meaning “for/because.” “La via lunga” is the subject, while “ne sospigne” uses the archaic/poetic pronoun “ne” with the sense of “us,” and “sospigne” means “urges/pushes on.”
segments.inferno-4-g25: “Così si mise” means “thus he set himself/moved off,” with reflexive “si” and “mise,” the passato remoto of “mettere.” “Mi fé intrare” is a causative construction: archaic “fé” = “fece,” “made/caused,” followed by the infinitive “intrare”; “cigne” is not “swan” here but an archaic form of “cingere,” “encircles/girds,” with the relative “che” referring to “cerchio.”
segments.inferno-4-g26: “Quivi” is a locative adverb meaning “there,” pointing to the place just entered. It sets the scene for the whole description that follows.
segments.inferno-4-g27: “Secondo che” forms an idiom meaning “according as” or “as far as,” with an implied verb such as “one could judge.” “Per ascoltare” means “by listening,” expressing the means by which Dante perceives what is happening.
segments.inferno-4-g28: “Non avea pianto” is an old impersonal/existential construction: literally “there had no weeping,” naturally “there was no weeping.” “Mai che” has a restrictive sense, “except/but,” and “che l’aura etterna facevan tremare” is a relative clause modifying “sospiri”; “facevan” is archaic “facevano,” “made.”
segments.inferno-4-g29: “Ciò avvenia” means “this happened/came about,” with “avvenia” an imperfect form. “Di duol sanza martìri” gives the cause or source: “from grief without torments,” where “sanza” is the old form of “senza.”
segments.inferno-4-g30: “Ch’avean le turbe” is a relative clause: “which the crowds had,” referring back to the grief just mentioned. “Avean” is an abbreviated imperfect form of “avevano.”
segments.inferno-4-g31: “Ch’eran” is another relative clause, this time describing “le turbe.” “Molte e grandi” agrees with the plural feminine “turbe,” meaning the crowds were numerous and large.
segments.inferno-4-g32: This phrase depends on “turbe,” specifying what the crowds were made up of: infants, women, and men. The repeated “di” gives a solemn, enumerative rhythm.
segments.inferno-4-g33: “Lo buon maestro” uses “lo” as an old form of the masculine article before “buon.” The verb of speaking is omitted but understood from context: “the good master [said] to me.”
segments.inferno-4-g34: “Tu non dimandi” is phrased as a negative question or reproach: “Do you not ask?” “Che spiriti son questi” has interrogative “che” meaning “what,” while the final “che tu vedi” is a relative clause, “that/whom you see.”
segments.inferno-4-g1: The verb comes first in the poetic form “Ruppemi,” meaning “it interrupted me,” with the enclitic pronoun “-mi” attached; its subject is delayed until “un greve truono.” “L’alto sonno” is the object, where “alto” means “deep,” and “ne la testa” is an archaic spelling of “nella testa,” “in the head.”
segments.inferno-4-g2: “Sì ch’” introduces a result clause: the thunder was so powerful that Dante woke up. “Mi riscossi” is passato remoto and reflexive, “I roused myself,” and “come persona ch’è per forza desta” compares him to someone who “is awakened by force,” with “ch’è” contracted from “che è.”
segments.inferno-4-g3: The clause continues the sequence of actions after waking. “L’occhio riposato” literally means “the rested eye,” but functions as “my rested gaze,” and “intorno mossi” has poetic word order for “I moved around.”
segments.inferno-4-g4: This is a compact participial phrase describing Dante’s posture while he looks around. “Dritto” means “upright,” and “levato” is a past participle meaning “risen” or “raised up.”
segments.inferno-4-g5: “Fiso” is used adverbially: he looked “fixedly” or “intently.” “Per conoscer” is “in order to know/recognize,” with the infinitive apocopated from “conoscere,” and “dov’ io fossi” is an indirect question with imperfect subjunctive “fossi,” meaning “where I was.”
segments.inferno-4-g6: “Vero è che” front-loads the predicate adjective and means “It is true that.” “’n su la proda” is a poetic contraction for “in su la proda,” “upon the edge/bank,” and “mi trovai” is reflexive in sense: “I found myself.” “De la” is archaic for “della,” while the relative “che” refers to the valley; “’ntrono” is a poetic noun meaning “thunder/roar,” the thing that the valley “accoglie,” and “d’infiniti guai” specifies that this roar is made of endless wails.
segments.inferno-4-g7: The adjectives “Oscura,” “profonda,” and “nebulosa” all describe the implied subject, the valley. The verb “era” is placed after the first two adjectives for poetic balance, and “tanto che” introduces the consequence: it was so dark and misty that Dante could not see into it.
segments.inferno-4-g8: “Per” plus the infinitive can have a concessive or instrumental force here, close to “even by” or “though I tried by.” “Ficcar lo viso a fondo” is vivid and idiomatic: literally “to stick the face/gaze to the bottom,” meaning to strain one’s sight deep down into the abyss.
segments.inferno-4-g9: “Vi” is locative, meaning “there,” referring back to the abyss. “Discernea” is an archaic/imperfect form of “discerneva,” “I could discern/make out,” and with “non” plus “alcuna cosa” it means “not anything at all.”
segments.inferno-4-g10: “Or” is shortened from “ora,” “now,” and “discendiam” is a shortened first-person plural form used hortatively: “let us descend.” “Qua giù” means “down here,” while “nel” contracts “in” + “il” and expresses motion into; “cieco mondo” is literally “blind world,” meaning the dark, lightless realm below.
segments.inferno-4-g11: “Cominciò” is passato remoto, “began,” here meaning “began to speak.” The subject “il poeta” follows the verb in normal literary inversion, and “tutto smorto” uses “tutto” adverbially, “completely/utterly pale.”
segments.inferno-4-g88: This segment contains only a period (full stop) punctuation mark. It has no lexical or grammatical content and merely marks the end of the sentence.
segments.inferno-4-g89: The verb of speaking is omitted, a common concise narrative construction: “And he to me” means “And he said/replied to me.” “Quelli” refers back to the speaker, Virgil, and “a me” marks Dante as the addressee.
segments.inferno-4-g90: “L’onrata nominanza” is the subject of the sentence: “the honored renown.” The relative clause “che di lor suona sù ne la tua vita” modifies that renown: literally, “which of them sounds/resounds up above in your life,” meaning the fame they have in the living world.
segments.inferno-4-g91: “Grazïa acquista in ciel” gives the main predicate: that renown “obtains/wins grace in heaven.” The following “che” is relative, referring to “grazïa,” and “sì li avanza” means that this grace so elevates or surpassingly distinguishes them; “li” functions as a pronoun, though its listed glosses do not include that use.
segments.inferno-4-g92: “Intanto” sets the action at the same time as the preceding exchange: “meanwhile.” “Voce fu ... udita” is a passive construction, with “fu” as auxiliary and “udita” as past participle; “per me” expresses the experiencer/agent, literally close to “through/by me.”
segments.inferno-4-g93: “Onorate” is a second-person plural imperative: the voice commands the listeners to “honor.” “L’altissimo poeta” means “the most exalted poet,” with the superlative adjective placed before/with the noun in a solemn style.
segments.inferno-4-g94: This is a simple main clause: “his shade returns.” “Ombra” in Dante often means the soul or spirit of a dead person, and “sua” refers to the poet just mentioned.
segments.inferno-4-g95: “Ch’” is a contracted relative pronoun, “which,” referring to “l’ombra.” “Era dipartita” is pluperfect in sense, formed with imperfect “era” plus past participle, meaning “had departed.”
segments.inferno-4-g96: “Poi che” functions together as a temporal conjunction, “after/when,” though literally it is “then that.” “Fu restata e queta” describes the voice as having stopped and become quiet; “fu” is auxiliary-like here, while “queta” is an adjective agreeing with “voce.”
segments.inferno-4-g97: “Vidi” governs an object plus infinitive construction: Dante “saw four great shades come/coming toward us.” “A noi” gives direction, “to/toward us,” and “grand’” is the apocopated form of “grandi” before “ombre.”
segments.inferno-4-g98: “Sembianz’” is an apocopated form of “sembianza,” here meaning “appearance” or “look.” “Avevan” is a shortened poetic form of “avevano,” and the paired “né ... né ...” gives “neither ... nor”: their expression was neither sad nor glad.
segments.inferno-4-g35: “Or” means “now.” “vo’” is an apocopated poetic form of “voglio,” “I want,” not the verb “to go”; it governs “che” plus the subjunctive “sappi,” “that you know.”
segments.inferno-4-g36: “innanzi che” is a temporal conjunction meaning “before.” “più andi” means “you go further”; “andi” is an archaic/poetic second-person singular present subjunctive used after “innanzi che.”
segments.inferno-4-g37: “ch’” introduces the thing Virgil wants Dante to know: “that.” “ei” is an archaic plural pronoun, “they,” and “peccaro” is a shortened poetic preterite form, “they sinned”; “non” negates it.
segments.inferno-4-g38: “s’elli” means “se elli,” “if they”; the apostrophe marks elision. “hanno mercedi” literally means “have rewards/merits,” referring to virtuous qualities or deserts that still do not save them.
segments.inferno-4-g39: “non basta” is impersonal here: “it is not enough” or “does not suffice.” The implied subject is their merits from the previous segment.
segments.inferno-4-g40: “perché” gives the reason: “because.” “ebber” is a shortened preterite plural of “ebbero,” and with “battesmo” it means “they had/received baptism.”
segments.inferno-4-g41: “ch’è” means “which is,” referring back to “battesmo.” “porta de la fede” means “the gate of the faith”; “de la” is the older spelling of modern “della,” and “che tu credi” is a relative clause, “that you believe/profess.”
segments.inferno-4-g42: “s’e’” means “se ei,” “if they”; both parts are contracted/poetic. “furon” is shortened “furono,” “they were,” and “dinanzi al cristianesmo” means temporally “before Christianity.”
segments.inferno-4-g43: “adorar” is not an infinitive here but a shortened third-person plural preterite, “they worshipped.” “debitamente” modifies the verb, “duly/rightly,” and “a Dio” marks the object of worship in an older construction.
segments.inferno-4-g44: “di questi cotai” means “of these such people,” with “di” expressing membership in the group just described. “son io medesmo” has the verb before the subject for emphasis: “I myself am.”
segments.inferno-4-g45: “Per” expresses cause: “because of” or “for.” “tai” is a poetic form of “tali,” “such,” and “difetti” here means lacks or deficiencies, especially the lack of baptism and proper worship just mentioned.
segments.inferno-4-g46: This phrase contrasts their actual fault with other sins: “not for any other evil/wickedness.” “rio” is used substantively, meaning something wicked or sinful, rather than a literal stream.
segments.inferno-4-g47: “semo” is an archaic form of “siamo,” “we are.” “perduti” means “lost” in the theological sense, that is, excluded from salvation or damned.
segments.inferno-4-g48: “sol” is poetic for “solo,” “only,” and “di tanto” means “to this extent / by so much.” The “che” introduces the result or measure of their harm: they live in desire or longing, but without hope.
segments.inferno-4-g49: The subject is “Gran duol,” “great sorrow,” placed first for emphasis. “mi prese al cor” literally means “seized me at the heart,” and “lo ’ntesi” means “I heard/understood it,” with “’ntesi” as an elided form of “intesi.”
segments.inferno-4-g50: “però che” means “because,” introducing the reason for Dante’s sorrow. “gente di molto valore” means “people of great worth,” and “che ’n quel limbo eran sospesi” is a relative clause: “who were held in suspense in that Limbo.”
segments.inferno-4-g51: “Dimmi” is the imperative “di’” plus the enclitic pronoun “mi”: “tell me.” The direct address begins Dante’s question to Virgil.
segments.inferno-4-g52: “maestro mio” is a vocative phrase, “my master/teacher.” The possessive follows the noun, a normal Italian order especially in address.
segments.inferno-4-g53: The repeated “dimmi” intensifies Dante’s request. It again combines the imperative “tell” with the object pronoun “me.”
segments.inferno-4-g54: “segnore” is an older spelling of “signore,” used here as respectful address to Virgil. It means “lord” or “sir,” not the divine title in this context.
segments.inferno-4-g55: “comincia’ io” is “cominciai io,” “I began,” with the final vowel elided for meter and the subject placed after the verb. “per voler esser certo” means “in order to / because I wished to be certain,” and “quella fede che vince ogne errore” means “that faith which conquers every error.”
segments.inferno-4-g56: “uscicci” combines the verb “uscì,” “went out,” with enclitic “ci,” “from here/there,” giving “did anyone ever leave from here?” “mai” means “ever” in the question, and “alcuno” is the postverbal subject, “anyone.”
segments.inferno-4-g57: The paired “o ... o ...” means “either ... or ... .” “per suo merto” means “by his own merit,” while “per altrui” means “by another’s merit,” with “altrui” used possessively.
segments.inferno-4-g58: “che” is a relative pronoun referring back to “alcuno,” “anyone.” “fosse” is imperfect subjunctive after the questioning construction, and “beato” is an adjective meaning “blessed,” describing one admitted to heavenly bliss.
segments.inferno-4-g172: The subject is “La sesta compagnia,” the group of six just mentioned; “sesta” is an ordinal adjective modifying “compagnia.” “In due si scema” uses reflexive/mediopassive “si” with present-tense “scema”: literally, the company “diminishes itself into two,” meaning it is reduced to two people.
segments.inferno-4-g173: The word order is poetic but clear: the subject “il savio duca” comes after the verb, while “mi” is the direct object before it. “Per altra via” means “by/along another way,” and “mena” is present tense, “leads.”
segments.inferno-4-g174: This phrase depends on “mena”: the guide leads him “fuor de la queta.” “Fuor de” is an archaic/poetic form for “out of/from,” and “la queta” is a substantivized feminine adjective, “the calm/quiet place or air.”
segments.inferno-4-g175: “Ne l’aura” is the older separated spelling of modern “nell’aura,” here with motion: “into the air.” “Che trema” is a relative clause modifying “aura”; “trema” is present indicative, giving the air an eerie, shaking quality.
segments.inferno-4-g176: “Vegno” is an archaic/poetic form of modern Italian “vengo,” present tense: “I come.” “In parte” means “into a place/region,” and “ove” introduces a relative clause of place; “non è che luca” is existential, literally “there is not [anything] that shines,” with “luca” as a present subjunctive form of “lucere,” not the name Luca.
segments.inferno-4-g76: “Non lasciavam” is an imperfect first-person plural form with the final -o of “lasciavamo” dropped; with the substantivized infinitive “l’andar,” it means “we were not leaving off/stopping our going.” “Perch’ ei dicessi” gives the reason or circumstance, with “ei” = “he” and “dicessi” an archaic/poetic form functioning as third-person imperfect subjunctive, equivalent to “dicesse,” “he was saying/speaking.”
segments.inferno-4-g77: “Ma” contrasts the preceding negative: they did not stop, but instead continued. “Passavam” is another imperfect first-person plural with the final -o omitted, and “tuttavia” here means “continually/all the while,” not the modern “nevertheless.”
segments.inferno-4-g78: The repeated “la selva” resumes and emphasizes the object just mentioned. It prepares for the corrective clarification that this is not an ordinary forest but a metaphorical one made up of spirits.
segments.inferno-4-g79: “Dico” is a parenthetical first-person present: “I say,” in the sense “I mean.” It signals that the poet is clarifying the metaphor of “selva.”
segments.inferno-4-g80: “Di spiriti spessi” is a genitive/specifying phrase depending on “selva”: a forest made “of” spirits. “Spessi” is plural adjective agreeing with “spiriti” and means “thick, dense, crowded.”
segments.inferno-4-g81: The predicate adjective “lunga” comes before the subject “la nostra via,” giving the sense “our way/journey was not yet long.” “Di qua dal sonno” is an idiom meaning “on this side of the sleep,” that is, after or since the sleep; “dal” contracts “da” + “il.”
segments.inferno-4-g82: “Quand’ io vidi” introduces the main event with “vidi,” the first-person singular passato remoto, “I saw.” “Ch’emisperio di tenebre vincia” is a relative clause modifying “foco”; “vincia” is an archaic/poetic imperfect form, meaning that the fire was overcoming or mastering a hemisphere of darkness.
segments.inferno-4-g83: “Di lungi” is a fronted adverbial expression meaning “from far off” or “at a distance.” “N’eravamo” contains elided “ne,” here “from it/there,” before “eravamo”; “ancora un poco” means “still a little” or “still somewhat.”
segments.inferno-4-g84: “Ma non sì che...” is a result construction: “but not so [far] that...”; the repeated “non” creates the sense “not so far that I could not discern.” “Discernessi” is imperfect subjunctive after “che,” and the following “ch’orrevol gente possedea quel loco” is the content of what Dante could partly perceive; “possedea” is an archaic imperfect for “possedeva.”
segments.inferno-4-g85: This is a vocative address: “O tu,” “O you.” “Ch’onori” is a relative clause referring to “tu,” with “onori” as second-person singular present, and “scïenzïa e arte” are its direct objects; the diaereses mark the word’s syllables for the meter.
segments.inferno-4-g86: “Questi chi son” places the demonstrative “questi” first for emphasis: “these—who are they?” “C’hanno” is elided “che hanno,” introducing a relative clause, and “cotanta” agrees with feminine “onranza,” meaning “such great honor.”
segments.inferno-4-g87: Here “che” refers back to the great honor and introduces the clause explaining its effect: it separates them from the others. “De li” is an archaic form of “degli,” while the second “li” is a direct-object pronoun, “them,” before the present verb “diparte.”
segments.inferno-4-g59: This segment contains only a full stop; it has no independent grammar or lexical content and merely closes the preceding utterance.
segments.inferno-4-g60: E links this answer to the preceding question. Quei means “he/the one” and is defined by the relative clause che ’ntese; ’ntese is an aphetic form of intese, “understood,” while il mio parlar is a substantivized infinitive meaning “my speech,” modified by coverto, an archaic form of coperto, here “concealed/veiled.”
segments.inferno-4-g61: Rispuose is an archaic form of rispose, the third-person singular past tense meaning “answered.” It is the main verb whose subject is quei in the previous segment.
segments.inferno-4-g62: Io era uses the imperfect of essere to give background: “I was.” Nuovo in questo stato means newly arrived or inexperienced in this condition/place, namely the state being described.
segments.inferno-4-g63: Quando introduces the temporal clause dependent on Io era: “when...” Vidi venire is a perception construction, “I saw ... come,” with un possente as the object of vidi and the understood subject of venire; ci is an adverbial “here/there, to this place.”
segments.inferno-4-g64: This prepositional phrase describes un possente. Coronato is a past participle used adjectivally, and con segno di vittoria means that he is marked or crowned with a sign of victory.
segments.inferno-4-g65: Trasseci is trasse + ci: the past-tense verb “drew/led out” with ci used adverbially as “from here,” not as the direct object. The direct object begins with l’ombra, “the shade,” and del primo parente identifies it as the shade of the “first parent,” Adam.
segments.inferno-4-g66: This continues the list of souls governed by Trasseci. D’Abèl suo figlio is elliptical for “the shade of Abel, his son,” while quella di Noè uses quella to stand in for l’ombra, “that/the one of Noah.”
segments.inferno-4-g67: This is another elliptical genitive phrase in the list: “of Moses.” Legista and ubidente are appositive adjectives/nouns describing Moses as lawgiver and obedient servant.
segments.inferno-4-g68: The names continue the same object list, with the repeated idea “the shade of” left unstated. Patrïarca and re are appositions after the proper names: “Abraham, patriarch,” and “David, king.”
segments.inferno-4-g69: Israèl here means Jacob, and the con phrases add those associated with him. Lo padre uses the older article lo where modern Italian would often have il; co’ is a contraction of con + i, and nati is a substantivized plural meaning “offspring/children.”
segments.inferno-4-g70: Per cui is a relative phrase referring to Rachel: “for whom.” Fé is a poetic shortened form of fece, past tense of fare, here meaning “did” or “endured/accomplished” so much for her.
segments.inferno-4-g71: This adds an indefinite group to the named list. Altri molti has the adjective/noun order of “many others,” with molti functioning substantively with altri.
segments.inferno-4-g72: The final e coordinates the result with the preceding action. Feceli is fece + li, an Old Italian enclitic construction meaning “made them,” and beati is a plural predicate adjective: “blessed.”
segments.inferno-4-g73: Vo’ is not andare here but a shortened form of voglio, “I want.” The first che introduces the object clause required by voglio, and sappi is the second-person singular present subjunctive of sapere; the second che introduces what is to be known.
segments.inferno-4-g74: Dinanzi ad essi is temporal here, meaning “before them,” rather than spatial “in front of them.” Ad is the euphonic form of a before a vowel, governed by dinanzi.
segments.inferno-4-g75: Spiriti umani is the plural subject, placed before the verb. Eran is a shortened form of erano, and eran salvati forms an imperfect passive; with non it means that human spirits were not, or had not yet been, saved.
segments.inferno-4-g116: "Così" links this movement to the honor just described, meaning "thus" or "in this way." "Andammo" is the first-person plural past historic of "andare," and "infino a" is an archaic/poetic form for "as far as, up to"; "la lumera" means the light toward which they walk.
segments.inferno-4-g117: "Parlando" is a gerund modifying the subject of "andammo": they went while speaking. "Cose che ’l tacere è bello" has a compressed poetic construction: literally, "things which the being-silent is fine," meaning matters about which silence is proper or beautiful; "’l" is the elided article "il" before the infinitive used as a noun.
segments.inferno-4-g118: "Sì com’" means "just as," with elision before "era." The adjective "bello" is understood from the previous clause: silence is fitting now, just as speaking was fitting "colà dov’ era," there in the place where that speech belonged or occurred.
segments.inferno-4-g119: "Venimmo" is a past-historic first-person plural form meaning "we came." "Al" contracts "a il," and "piè" is the apocopated poetic form of "piede," here meaning the base or foot of the castle; "d’un" contracts "di un."
segments.inferno-4-g120: This segment describes the castle rather than starting a new finite clause. "Sette volte" means "seven times," and "cerchiato" is a past participle used adjectivally, with "d’alte mura" expressing the means or agent: encircled by high walls.
segments.inferno-4-g121: "Difeso" is another past participle agreeing with "castello," continuing the description: it is defended or guarded. "Intorno" means "all around," and "d’un bel fiumicello" gives what protects it, with "d’" again functioning like "by."
segments.inferno-4-g122: "Questo" is fronted as the direct object, referring to the little river just mentioned: "this we crossed." "Passammo" is past historic, and "come terra dura" is an elliptical comparison, "as if it were hard ground."
segments.inferno-4-g123: "Per sette porte" places the route first: "through seven gates." "Intrai" is first-person singular past historic, shifting from "we" to Dante himself, while "con questi savi" identifies his companions as "these sages."
segments.inferno-4-g124: "Giugnemmo" is an archaic form of "giungemmo," past historic first-person plural, meaning "we arrived" or "we reached." "In prato" lacks the modern article and means "in/into a meadow," while "di fresca verdura" describes the meadow as full of fresh green growth.
segments.inferno-4-g125: "Genti" is the plural subject placed before the existential phrase "v’eran," where "v’" is elided "vi" meaning "there" and "eran" is poetic for "erano." "Con occhi tardi e gravi" describes their manner through their eyes: slow, restrained, and solemn.
segments.inferno-4-g126: This prepositional phrase continues describing the people. "Di grande autorità" means "of great authority" or dignity, while "ne’" is a contraction of "in i/nei," and "lor" is the poetic possessive "their."
segments.inferno-4-g127: "Parlavan" is the truncated poetic form of "parlavano," imperfect indicative, describing their habitual or characteristic way of speaking. "Rado" is adverbial here, meaning "seldom" or "rarely."
segments.inferno-4-g128: "Con voci soavi" completes the description of how they spoke. The prepositional phrase uses "con" instrumentally or manner-wise: they spoke with gentle, sweet voices.
segments.inferno-4-g129: “Traemmoci” is an archaic first-person plural past form with enclitic “-ci”: literally “we drew ourselves away,” i.e. “we withdrew.” “Da l’un de’ canti” means “from one of the corners,” with “de’” contracted from “de li/dei,” “of the.”
segments.inferno-4-g130: “In loco aperto” gives the destination or resulting position after the withdrawal: “into/in an open place.” “Loco” is an archaic or poetic form for modern Italian “luogo.”
segments.inferno-4-g131: “Luminoso e alto” continues to describe “loco,” with two coordinated adjectives after the noun. “Alto” here means physically elevated or high, not “deep.”
segments.inferno-4-g132: “Sì che” introduces a result clause: “so that.” “Veder si potien” uses an impersonal/passive construction with archaic “potien” for “potean/potevano”: literally “one could see,” meaning “they could be seen”; “tutti quanti” is an emphatic “all of them.”
segments.inferno-4-g133: “Colà” is a deictic adverb meaning “there/yonder,” pointing to the place just reached. “Diritto” functions adverbially here, “straight” or “directly,” not as the noun “right/law.”
segments.inferno-4-g134: “Sovra ’l” is poetic for “sopra il,” meaning “on/upon the.” “Verde smalto” literally suggests “green enamel,” a poetic image for smooth green turf or grass.
segments.inferno-4-g135: “Mi” is an indirect object, “to me.” “Fuor” here is not the adverb “outside,” but a shortened poetic form of “furono,” “were”; thus “mi fuor mostrati” means “were shown to me,” and “li spiriti magni” is the subject placed after the verb.
segments.inferno-4-g136: “Che” refers back to the great spirits or to the sight of them: “at the seeing of whom/which.” “Del vedere” is a verbal noun phrase, “from/for the seeing,” and “m’essalto” is “mi essalto,” a reflexive present form meaning “I exalt myself” or “I feel uplifted”; “in me stesso” means inwardly, “within myself.”
segments.inferno-4-g137: “I’” is an elided poetic form of “Io,” “I,” not the article “i.” “Vidi” is the first-person singular passato remoto, “I saw,” taking “Eletra” as its object; “con molti compagni” adds those accompanying her.
segments.inferno-4-g138: “Tra ’ quai” means “among whom,” referring back to Electra’s many companions. “Conobbi” is passato remoto and here means “I recognized,” followed by the direct objects “Ettòr ed Enea.”
segments.inferno-4-g139: “Cesare” continues the list of figures Dante saw or recognized. “Armato” is a past participle used adjectivally, “armed,” and “con li occhi grifagni” describes Caesar as having sharp, hawk-like eyes.
segments.inferno-4-g140: “Vidi” again is passato remoto, “I saw,” introducing more direct objects in Dante’s catalogue. The article “la” before “Pantasilea” is the common Italian use of the definite article before some female names or epithets.
segments.inferno-4-g141: “Da l’altra parte” is a locative phrase meaning “on the other side.” “Che” introduces a relative clause modifying “’l re Latino,” and “sedea” is an archaic imperfect form of “sedeva,” “was sitting”; “con Lavina sua figlia” means “with Lavinia, his daughter.”
segments.inferno-4-g142: "Vidi" is the first-person singular past tense, "I saw," beginning another item in Dante's catalogue. "Quel Bruto" means "that Brutus," specified by the relative clause "che cacciò Tarquino," where "che" means "who" and "cacciò" is a simple past verb meaning "expelled."
segments.inferno-4-g143: "Lucrezia" is a direct object coordinated with "Bruto" after the governing verb "Vidi." The name is simply listed without a repeated verb, as is common in poetic catalogues.
segments.inferno-4-g144: "Iulia" is another direct object of the earlier "Vidi." The Latinizing spelling marks the Roman name Julia.
segments.inferno-4-g145: "Marzïa e Corniglia" completes the coordinated list of Roman women seen by Dante. "E" joins the two names, while "Corniglia" is the Italian form of Cornelia, not the place-name Corniglia here.
segments.inferno-4-g146: "E" starts a new coordinated clause after the semicolon. "Solo" describes Saladin as being alone or set apart, not merely as "only."
segments.inferno-4-g147: "In parte" is an adverbial phrase meaning "in a place apart" or "off to one side." It reinforces "solo," explaining Saladin's separated position in the scene.
segments.inferno-4-g148: "Vidi" is again the first-person past verb, now with "'l Saladino" as its object. "'l" is the elided form of "il," the definite article before the proper name.
segments.inferno-4-g149: "Poi ch'" functions as a temporal conjunction, "after" or "when," with elision of "che" before a vowel. "Innalzai" is first-person simple past, and "un poco più le ciglia" literally means "the eyelids a little more," idiomatically "my eyes a little higher."
segments.inferno-4-g150: "Vidi 'l maestro" gives the main clause: "I saw the master," referring to Aristotle. "Di color che sanno" means "of those who know," with "color" as an archaic contraction for "coloro"; "seder" is an infinitive dependent on "vidi," meaning "I saw him sitting among a philosophical company."
segments.inferno-4-g151: "Tutti" is the plural subject, "all," referring to the philosophical company. "Lo" is the object pronoun "him," pointing back to Aristotle, and "miran" is a shortened poetic form of "mirano," "they gaze at."
segments.inferno-4-g152: This repeats the subject "tutti" for emphasis. "Onor li fanno" is an idiom meaning "they do him honor"; "li" is an old dative form meaning "to him," though its listed modern article sense does not fit here.
segments.inferno-4-g153: "Quivi" means "there," locating the next figures within the scene just described. "Vid' ïo" is an inverted and elided form of "vidi io," "I saw," used for meter before the names Socrates and Plato.
segments.inferno-4-g154: "Che" introduces a relative clause referring to Socrates and Plato. "'Nnanzi a li altri" means "before the others," while "più presso li stanno" means "they stand closer to him," with the second "li" again functioning as an old dative pronoun, "to him."
segments.inferno-4-g155: "Democrito" is another object in the continuing list governed by Dante's seeing. The relative clause "che 'l mondo a caso pone" describes his doctrine: "who posits the world as due to chance," with "a caso" meaning "by chance."
segments.inferno-4-g156: "Dïogenès" is a single-name item in the catalogue. The diaeresis marks the word's syllables for the verse.
segments.inferno-4-g157: "Anassagora e Tale" adds two more philosophers, joined by "e." "Tale" here is the proper name Thales, not the ordinary Italian adjective "such," so none of the listed senses fits.
segments.inferno-4-g158: "Empedoclès" continues the list as another direct object understood with the earlier verb of seeing. The accent and spelling reflect Dante's poetic form of the Greek name Empedocles.
segments.inferno-4-g159: "Eraclito e Zenone" are two more coordinated names in the catalogue. The semicolon closes this sub-list of philosophers before Dante turns to further learned figures.
segments.inferno-4-g160: "E vidi" resumes the explicit verb "I saw" after the long list. "Il buono accoglitor del quale" means "the good collector of qualities," a periphrasis for Dioscorides; "quale" here has the old sense of qualities or properties, not one of the listed modern senses.
segments.inferno-4-g161: "Dïascoride dico" clarifies the preceding periphrasis: "Dioscorides, I mean." The verb "dico" is present tense and here has the parenthetical sense "I mean."
segments.inferno-4-g162: "E vidi" again restarts the catalogue with a finite verb. "Orfeo" is the direct object, the mythical poet Orpheus.
segments.inferno-4-g163: "Tulïo e Lino e Seneca morale" continues as a coordinated list after "vidi." "Tulïo" is Tully, that is Cicero, and "morale" qualifies Seneca as a moral philosopher or moral writer.
segments.inferno-4-g164: "Euclide geomètra" places an appositive title after the name: "Euclid the geometer." "E Tolomeo" then adds Ptolemy as another object in the same catalogue.
segments.inferno-4-g165: "Ipocràte" is another single-name item, Hippocrates. It remains grammatically dependent on the earlier "vidi."
segments.inferno-4-g166: "Avicenna e Galïeno" gives two further learned medical authorities, joined by "e." Both names continue the same object list.
segments.inferno-4-g167: "Averoìs" is Averroes, introduced as the final figure in this catalogue. The following relative clause will identify him more specifically.
segments.inferno-4-g168: "Che" introduces a relative clause referring to Averroes. "'L gran comento feo" means "made/wrote the great commentary," with "feo" as an archaic past-tense form of "fare."
segments.inferno-4-g169: "Io non posso" is the first-person present negative, "I cannot." "Ritrar di tutti a pieno" means "depict or describe all of them fully," with "ritrar" an apocopated infinitive and "a pieno" an adverbial phrase meaning "completely."
segments.inferno-4-g170: "Però che" is an old causal conjunction meaning "because," not the modern adversative "però" = "however." The subject follows the verb: "il lungo tema" is what "caccia" or drives Dante onward, while "sì" sets up the following result clause, "so ... that."
segments.inferno-4-g171: This is the result clause introduced by the earlier "sì": "so that many times..." "Al fatto il dir vien meno" has an inverted order: literally, "to the fact the saying comes less," meaning that speech fails or falls short of the reality.
segments.inferno-4-g99: “Lo” is an archaic form of the masculine singular article, equivalent here to modern “il.” “Cominciò a dire” uses the passato remoto followed by “a” + infinitive, meaning “began to say”; the subject is “lo buon maestro,” Virgil.
segments.inferno-4-g100: “Mira” is a singular imperative, addressed by Virgil to Dante: “look at.” “Colui” points to a specific person, and “con quella spada in mano” is a descriptive prepositional phrase, with “in mano” meaning “in his hand.”
segments.inferno-4-g101: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring back to “colui.” “Vien” is the apocopated present form of “viene,” and “dinanzi ai tre” means “in front of the three”; “sì come” is a poetic comparative expression, “just as / like.”
segments.inferno-4-g102: “Quelli” is an archaic demonstrative pronoun meaning “that one,” referring to the figure just described. The predicate identifies him as “Omero,” with “poeta sovrano” in apposition, meaning the supreme or ruling poet.
segments.inferno-4-g103: “L’altro” means “the other,” continuing the enumeration of the poets. “Che vene” is a relative clause, with “vene” an archaic form of “viene,” “comes”; “satiro” here means “satirist,” not a mythological satyr.
segments.inferno-4-g104: The word order is straightforward: “Ovidio” is the subject, “è” is the copula, and “’l terzo” means “the third.” The elided article “’l” is the poetic shortened form of “il.”
segments.inferno-4-g105: This final phrase elliptically continues the previous construction: “and the last [is] Lucan.” The verb “è” is understood from the preceding clause, a common compressed poetic structure.
segments.inferno-4-g106: “Però che” functions as a causal conjunction here, meaning “because,” though “però” by itself often means “therefore” or “however.” “Ciascun meco si convene” means “each agrees/is joined with me,” and “nel nome” refers to the shared title or name; in “che sonò la voce sola,” the relative “che” is the object sounded or uttered by “la voce sola,” whose subject follows the verb.
segments.inferno-4-g107: “Fannomi” combines “fanno” + enclitic “mi,” a poetic ordering meaning “they do/make to me.” The expression “fare onore” means “to do honor” or “to pay honor.”
segments.inferno-4-g108: “Di ciò” means “in this / with respect to this,” referring back to their honoring him. “Fanno bene” is the idiom “they do well” or “they act rightly.”
segments.inferno-4-g109: “Vid’ i’” is an elided poetic form of “vidi io,” “I saw.” After this verb of perception, “adunar” is an infinitive meaning “to gather/assemble,” with “la bella scola” as the group seen gathering; “di quel segnor de l’altissimo canto” identifies it as the school or company belonging to Homer, the master of the highest poetry. The final relative clause, “che sovra li altri com’ aquila vola,” refers to that “segnor”: he flies above the others like an eagle.
segments.inferno-4-g110: “Da ch’” means “after” or “when,” introducing a temporal clause. “Ebber ragionato” is passato remoto of the auxiliary “avere” plus past participle, equivalent to “had discussed/talked,” and “alquanto” means “for some time” or “a while.”
segments.inferno-4-g111: “Volsersi” is a reflexive/passive-like passato remoto form meaning “they turned themselves.” “A me” marks the direction of their turning, while “con salutevol cenno” describes the manner: with a gracious or welcoming sign/gesture.
segments.inferno-4-g112: “’L mio maestro” uses the elided article “’l” before “mio maestro.” “Sorrise di tanto” means “smiled at so much / at this,” with “di” expressing the cause or occasion of the smile.
segments.inferno-4-g113: “E più d’onore ancora assai” places the object phrase before the verb for emphasis: “and much more honor still.” “Mi fenno” is an archaic form of “mi fecero,” here meaning “they did to me” or “they paid me.”
segments.inferno-4-g114: “Ch’” here explains the preceding statement, essentially “for/because.” In “ch’e’ sì mi fecer,” “e’” is not the conjunction “and” but an archaic pronoun meaning “they”; “mi fecer de la loro schiera” means “they made me of their company,” that is, counted me among their band.
segments.inferno-4-g115: “Sì ch’” is a result conjunction meaning “so that.” “Fui sesto” means “I was sixth,” because Dante is added to the five poets, and “tra cotanto senno” means “among so much wisdom,” referring reverently to the intellectual greatness of the group.

sentences.inferno-4-s5: “I will be first, and you will be second.”
sentences.inferno-4-s6: And I, who had noticed his pallor, said: “How shall I go on, if you are afraid, you who are usually a comfort to my doubting?”
sentences.inferno-4-s8: And he said to me: “The anguish of the people who are down here paints on my face that pity which you perceive as fear.”
sentences.inferno-4-s9: Let us go, for the long road urges us on.”
sentences.inferno-4-s10: Thus he set out, and thus he made me enter the first circle that girds the abyss.
sentences.inferno-4-s11: There, as far as one could tell by listening, there was no weeping except sighs, which made the eternal air tremble; this came from grief without torments, a grief felt by the crowds, which were many and vast, of infants and of women and of men.
sentences.inferno-4-s12: The good master said to me: “Do you not ask what spirits these are that you see?”
sentences.inferno-4-s1: A heavy thunderclap interrupted the deep sleep in my head, so that I roused myself like a person who is awakened by force; and I moved my rested gaze around, having risen upright, and looked intently to recognize the place where I was.
sentences.inferno-4-s2: It is true that I found myself upon the edge of the sorrowful valley of the abyss, which gathers the thunder of infinite wails.
sentences.inferno-4-s3: It was dark and deep and misty, so that, though I thrust my gaze down to the depths, I could not make out anything there.
sentences.inferno-4-s4: “Now let us descend down here into the blind world,” the poet began, utterly pale.
sentences.inferno-4-s26: And he said to me: “The honored renown of them that resounds above in your life wins grace in Heaven, which so advances them.”
sentences.inferno-4-s27: Meanwhile a voice was heard by me: “Honor the most exalted poet; his shade returns, which had departed.”
sentences.inferno-4-s28: After the voice had ceased and fallen quiet, I saw four great shades coming toward us; their appearance was neither sad nor joyful.
sentences.inferno-4-s13: Now I want you to know, before you go any farther, that they did not sin; and if they have merits, that is not enough, because they did not receive baptism, which is the gate of the faith that you believe; and if they lived before Christianity, they did not worship God rightly: and I myself am one of these such people.
sentences.inferno-4-s14: For such lacks, and not for any other wickedness, we are lost, and harmed only so far as this: that without hope we live in longing.
sentences.inferno-4-s15: Great sorrow seized me at the heart when I heard this, because I recognized people of great worth who were suspended in that Limbo.
sentences.inferno-4-s16: “Tell me, my master, tell me, my lord,” I began, wishing to be certain about that faith which conquers every error: “did anyone ever leave from here, either by his own merit or by another’s, who afterward was blessed?”
sentences.inferno-4-s45: The company of six dwindles to two: by another path the wise guide leads me, out of the calm, into the air that trembles.
sentences.inferno-4-s46: And I come into a place where there is nothing that shines.
sentences.inferno-4-s21: We did not stop going because he was speaking, but kept passing through the forest all the while—the forest, I mean, of crowded spirits.
sentences.inferno-4-s22: Our way on this side of the sleep was not yet long, when I saw a fire that overcame a hemisphere of darkness.
sentences.inferno-4-s23: We were still somewhat far from it, but not so far that I did not partly discern that honorable people occupied that place.
sentences.inferno-4-s24: “O you who honor knowledge and art, who are these who have such great honor that it sets them apart from the condition of the others?”
sentences.inferno-4-s18: And he who understood my veiled speech answered: 'I was new to this state when I saw a mighty one come here, crowned with the sign of victory.'
sentences.inferno-4-s19: He drew forth from here the shade of the first parent, of Abel his son and that of Noah, of Moses the lawgiver and obedient one; Abraham the patriarch and David the king, Israel with his father and with his offspring and with Rachel, for whom he did so much, and many others, and he made them blessed.
sentences.inferno-4-s20: And I want you to know that, before them, no human spirits had been saved.
sentences.inferno-4-s33: Thus we went as far as the light, speaking of things about which it is fitting to be silent, just as speaking was fitting there where it took place.
sentences.inferno-4-s34: We came to the foot of a noble castle, seven times encircled by high walls, guarded all around by a fair little stream.
sentences.inferno-4-s35: We crossed this as though it were solid ground; through seven gates I entered with these sages; we came into a meadow of fresh greenery.
sentences.inferno-4-s36: There were people there with slow and solemn eyes, of great authority in their countenances; they spoke seldom, with gentle voices.
sentences.inferno-4-s37: Thus we withdrew from one of the corners into an open place, bright and high, so that all of them could be seen.
sentences.inferno-4-s38: There, straight before us, upon the green turf, the great spirits were shown to me, and for having seen them I exalt myself within myself.
sentences.inferno-4-s39: I saw Electra with many companions, among whom I recognized Hector and Aeneas, and Caesar armed, with hawk-like eyes.
sentences.inferno-4-s40: I saw Camilla and Penthesilea; on the other side I saw King Latinus, who was sitting with Lavinia his daughter.
sentences.inferno-4-s41: I saw that Brutus who drove out Tarquin, Lucretia, Julia, Marcia and Cornelia; and, alone in a place apart, I saw Saladin.
sentences.inferno-4-s42: After I had raised my eyes a little higher, I saw the master of those who know sitting among the philosophical company.
sentences.inferno-4-s43: All gaze upon him, all do him honor: there I saw Socrates and Plato, who stand nearest to him before the others; Democritus, who makes the world depend on chance, Diogenes, Anaxagoras and Thales, Empedocles, Heraclitus and Zeno; and I saw the good collector of qualities, Dioscorides I mean; and I saw Orpheus, Tully and Linus and moral Seneca; Euclid the geometer and Ptolemy, Hippocrates, Avicenna and Galen, Averroes, who wrote the great commentary.
sentences.inferno-4-s44: I cannot describe them all fully, because the long theme drives me on so that many times speech falls short of the fact.
sentences.inferno-4-s29: The good master began to say: “Look at that one with that sword in his hand, who comes before the three like a lord: that one is Homer, the sovereign poet; the other who comes is Horace the satirist; Ovid is the third, and Lucan the last.”
sentences.inferno-4-s30: Because each of them agrees with me in the title that the lone voice sounded, they do me honor, and in that they do well.”
sentences.inferno-4-s31: Thus I saw the fair school of that lord of the loftiest song gather together, he who flies above the others like an eagle.
sentences.inferno-4-s32: After they had talked together for a while, they turned to me with a welcoming gesture, and my master smiled at that; and they did me still much more honor, for they made me one of their company, so that I was sixth among so much wisdom.
